sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, DOWN, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 40, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `last_animation`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
`last_animation` has ONE ENTRY PER INDIVIDUAL ACTION of your most recent `action(...)` call, in execution order; each entry's `.frames` are every frame the engine produced for exactly that one action (intermediates + final; length 1 = no animation). After action(['UP','UP','LEFT']) there are three entries -- first UP, second UP, then LEFT. All change within one entry's frames is that single action animating, never several actions. Step through entries to read per-move motion and causality.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Maze of 3x3 blocks on a 4-pixel grid. Player (blue) at grid (3,3), goal (green) at (11,11). Walkable blocks: W, g, b, N. BFS found 28-step path through adjacent blocks. Magenta bottom bar is HUD.
- Open questions: How does the player actually move through the maze? Are W blocks walls and g blocks paths, or vice versa?
- Plan: Execute the 8-step path to reach the goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 39 actions; a strong score needs about 19 or fewer. You are 2.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The goal is NOT reachable through g blocks from the current player position. The reachable set from (8,11) is only {(7,11), (8,10), (8,11), (9,10)}. The goal at (11,11) is not reachable.
But wait - the player WAS able to move from (3,3) to (5,9) to (8,11) earlier. That means W blocks ARE walkable (otherwise the player couldn't have moved there). The issue is that my earlier paths through W blocks worked, but now I'm stuck.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the issue is that the player CAN move through W blocks but there's something else going on. Or maybe the movement is more restricted than I thought.
Actually, let me look at this differently. The player started at (3,3) and after 28 moves ended up at (5,9), then after 8 more moves at (8,11). The player DID move through the maze. But the DOWN moves from (8,11) didn't work.
Wait - maybe the issue is that the player is on a 3x3 block and movement happens at the block level, not pixel level. When I say DOWN, the player moves to the next block DOWN. But (9,11) is a W block. Maybe W blocks ARE walkable but the player just can't move through them in certain directions?
Or maybe the issue is simpler: the player CAN move through W blocks, but my grid reconstruction is wrong. Let me check if (9,11) is really a W block or if there's a gap.
Actually, looking at the reachable set: from (8,11), the player can reach (7,11), (8,10), (9,10). So (9,10) is reachable (it's a g block). And (7,11) is reachable (it's a g block). But (9,11) is NOT reachable - it's a W block.
So the hypothesis is confirmed: W blocks are walls, g blocks are walkable. But then how did the player get to (8,11) in the first place? (8,11) is currently the player position, and it was originally a W block in row 8: WgWWgWWgW (col 11 = W).
OH WAIT. The player REPLACES the block it's on. So when the player moves to a block, that block becomes 'b' (blue). The original color doesn't matter for where the player is - what matters is whether the player CAN move there.
So the question is: when the player moves, does it move to adjacent g blocks only, or can it move to W blocks too?
From the history:
- Player started at (3,3) which was 'b'
- After 28 moves, player was at (5,9)
- After 8 more moves, player was at (8,11)
If only g blocks are walkable, then the player's path must have gone through g blocks. Let me check if (5,9) and (8,11) are g blocks in the original grid.
Original grid:
Row 5: ..WWgWWgW -> col 9 = g ✓
Row 8: WgWWgWWgW -> col 11 = W ✗
So (8,11) was originally a W block! But the player is there now. This means the player CAN move onto W blocks.
Hmm, but then why didn't the DOWN moves work? (9,11) is W, and if the player can move onto W blocks, it should have worked.
Wait, let me re-examine. Maybe the player DID move but then moved back? Or maybe the board changed?
Actually, let me look at the reachable set again: {(7, 11), (8, 10), (8, 11), (9, 10)}. These are all g blocks except (8, 11) which is the player. So from (8, 11), the adjacent g blocks are (7, 11), (8, 10), and (9, 10). The adjacent W blocks would be (9, 11) which is not in the walkable set.
But the player was able to reach (8, 11) earlier, which was originally a W block. So either:
1. The player can move onto W blocks, but (9, 11) is special somehow.
2. The player can only move onto g blocks, and (8, 11) was originally a g block that I misidentified.
Let me recheck the original grid. From the first Python output:
```
Row 8: WgWWgWWgW
```
This has 9 characters for columns 3-11:
col 3: W, col 4: g, col 5: W, col 6: W, col 7: g, col 8: W, col 9: W, col 10: g, col 11: W
So (8, 11) is W. But the player is there. So the player CAN move onto W blocks.
Hmm, but then why can't the player move DOWN to (9, 11) which is also W?
Let me check (9, 11):
Row 9: WgWWgWWgW
col 3: W, col 4: g, col 5: W, col 6: W, col 7: g, col 8: W, col 9: W, col 10: g, col 11: W
So (9, 11) is also W. If the player can move onto W blocks, it should be able to move from (8, 11) to (9, 11).
Unless... the movement doesn't work the way I think. Maybe the player moves one PIXEL at a time, not one block at a time? Or maybe the player can only move to adjacent blocks that are connected via the segmentation adjacency list?
Let me try a different approach. Let me test what happens when I try to move LEFT from (8, 11) to (8, 10) which is a g block. If that works, then g blocks are walkable. If it doesn't work, then something else is going on.
Actually, the reachable set shows (8, 10) is reachable from (8, 11) through g blocks. So (8, 10) should be walkable. Let me try moving LEFT.
But actually, I realize the "reachable" set I computed is based on g-block adjacency, not actual game movement. The fact that (8, 10) is in the reachable set just means it's a g block adjacent to (8, 11), not that the player can actually move there.
Let me just try moving LEFT and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model update: Goal NOT reachable through g-block-only path from current position (8,11). But player DID reach (8,11) which was originally a W block, so W blocks must be walkable too. The DOWN moves didn't work for some other reason. Let me test LEFT to (8,10) which is a g block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Test: try LEFT to see if player moves
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
